formulas.context_text: spatial structure, so that the mean degree of inbreeding does not depend on the parental source of alleles.) Summing up the three ways by which identity-by-descent can arise between uniting gametes, $$ \begin{aligned}f_{t}&=\left(\frac{1}{N_{e}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{N_{e}}\right)f_{t-1}+\left(1-\frac{1}{N_{e}}\right)f_{t-1}\\&=\frac{1}{2N_{e}}+\left(1-\frac{1}{2N_{e}}\right)f_{t-1}\end{aligned} $$ (3.1)

formulas.label: Formula 3.2
formulas.context_text: 1) $ total ways by which gametes can unite by self-fertilization. Because a total of $ N_{t-1}\mu_k $ successful gametes are produced, the expected fraction of zygotes derived from the same parent is $$ P_{t}=\frac{1}{N_{e}}=\frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{N_{t-1}}k_{i}(k_{i}-1)}{N_{t-1}\mu_{k}(N_{t-1}\mu_{k}-1)} $$ (3.2) where the denominator is the total number of pairs of uniting gametes necessary to produce the next generation. This expression can be simplified greatly by noting that $ \sum_{i=1}^{N_{t-1}} k_i(k_i

formulas.label: Formula 3.3
formulas.context_text: (k_i - 1)/N_{t-1} = E(k^2) - \mu_k = \sigma_k^2 + \mu_k(\mu_k - 1) $, and that because all zygotes are derived from two gametes, $ N_{t-1}\mu_k = 2N_t $. Substituting into Equation 3.2 and inverting, $$ N_{e}=\frac{2N_{t}-1}{(\sigma_{k}^{2}/\mu_{k})+\mu_{k}-1} $$ (3.3)

formulas.label: Formula 3.4
formulas.context_text: Equation 3.2 simplifies greatly under a number of conditions. For example, populations that are stable in size have, on average, two successful gametes per parent ( $ \mu_k = 2 $), leading to $$ N_{e}=\frac{4N-2}{\sigma_{k}^{2}+2} $$ (3.4)

formulas.label: Formula 3.5
formulas.context_text: $ ways of drawing gene pairs (the 4 because each parent carries two genes). Therefore, the probability that a generation- $ t $ individual carries a pair of genes derived from the same grandparent is $$ P_{t}=\frac{1}{N_{e}}=\frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{N_{t-2}}k_{i}(k_{i}-1)}{N_{t-2}\mu_{k}(N_{t-2}\mu_{k}-2)} $$ (3.5)

formulas.label: Formula 3.6
formulas.context_text: Employing the same kinds of substitutions used for Equation 3.3, $$ N_{e}=\frac{2(N_{t-1}-1)}{(\sigma_{k}^{2}/\mu_{k})+\mu_{k}-1} $$ (3.6) and for constant N (which implies $ \mu_{k}=2 $), $$ N_{e}=\frac{4(N-1)}{\sigma_{k}^{2}+2} $$ (3.7)

formulas.label: Formula 3.7
formulas.context_text: Employing the same kinds of substitutions used for Equation 3.3, $$ N_{e}=\frac{2(N_{t-1}-1)}{(\sigma_{k}^{2}/\mu_{k})+\mu_{k}-1} $$ (3.6) and for constant N (which implies $ \mu_{k}=2 $), $$ N_{e}=\frac{4(N-1)}{\sigma_{k}^{2}+2} $$ (3.7)

formulas.label: Formula 3.8

However, with selfing permitted, $$ N_{e}=\frac{N_{t-1}}{\left(4\sigma_{o,p}/\mu_{k}^{2}\right)+1} $$ (3.8) where $ \sigma_{o,p} $ is the covariance of the numbers of successful male $ (p,\mathrm{pollen}) $ and female $ (o,\mathrm{ovule}) $ gametes per parent (Crow and Denniston 1988). If $ \sigma_{o,p} $

formulas.context_text: shared ancestor with probability 0.5, the probability that O inherits both genes from the shared grandparent is 1/4. Thus, the total probability that O inherits two genes from the same grandparent is $$ P=\frac{1}{N_{e}}=\frac{1}{4N_{em}}+\frac{1}{4N_{ef}} $$ (3.9)

formulas.label: Formula 3.10

Skipping the intermediate steps, we simply note that $$ N_{es}=\frac{\mu_{sk}N_{s,t-2}-1}{\left(\sigma_{sk}^{2}/\mu_{sk}\right)+\mu_{sk}-1} $$ (3.10) where $s$ denotes the sex $(m$ or $f)$, and $\mu_{sk}$ and $\sigma_{sk}^{2}$ are the mean and variance of gamete production by sex $s$ (Crow and Denniston 1988). Latter (1959) provided a more elabora

Letting $\phi$ be the sex ratio (proportion of females), $$ N_{em}=\frac{4N_{m,t-2}}{2+\sigma_{mm}^{2}+\frac{2(1-\phi)}{\phi}\sigma_{mm,mf}+\left(\frac{1-\phi}{\phi}\right)^{2}\sigma_{mf}^{2}} $$ (3.11a) $$ N_{ef}=\frac{4N_{f,t-2}}{2+\sigma_{ff}^{2}+\frac{2\phi}{1-\phi}\sigma_{fm,ff}+\left(\frac{\phi}{1-\phi}\right)^{2}\sigma_{fm}^{2}} $$ (3.11b) where for male parents, $ \sigma_{mm}^{2} $ is the var

For an ideal monocious population, $$ T=\frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\tau}i\ell_{i}b_{i}}{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\tau}\ell_{i}b_{i}} $$ (3.13) where $ \ell_i $ is the probability of surviving to age $ i $, $ b_i $ is the expected number of offspring produced by parents of age $ i $, and $ \tau $ is the maximum reproductive age. The quantity

For a dioecious population, $ T $ is further complicated by the need to average over males $ (m) $ and females $ (f) $, $$ T=\frac{T_{mm}+T_{mf}+T_{fm}+T_{ff}}{4} $$ (3.14) where $ T_{mf} $, for example, is the average age of male parents of daughters. The average generation length is the natural time scale for the evolutionary analysis of age-structured populations. Le

Again making the assumption that the population is stable in terms of size, sex ratio, and age composition, the effective size of an age-structured population with separate sexes is $$ N_{e}=\frac{N_{eb}T}{1+\left(1-\phi_{b}\right)\sum_{i=1}^{\tau_{f}}\left(\frac{1}{\ell_{i+1}^{f}}-\frac{1}{\ell_{i}^{f}}\right)\left(\sum_{f}\right)^{2}+\phi_{b}\sum_{i=1}^{\tau_{m}}\left(\frac{1}{\ell_{i+1}^{m}}-\frac{1}{\ell_{i}^{m}}\right)\left(\sum_{m}\right)^{2}} $$ (3.15) where $ (\sum_s)^2 = \left( \sum_{j \geq i+1} \ell_j^s b_j^s \right)^2 $, $ \tau_s $ is the maximum age of reproduction for sex $ s $, and $ s = m $ or $ f $ (Emigh and Pollak 1979). Equating the exponents of these two expressions, $$ N_{e}^{*}\simeq\frac{t}{1/N_{e,0}+1/N_{e,1}+\cdots+1/N_{e,t-1}} $$ (3.18)
